{{Short description|American academic}}
{{Infobox academic
| name               = Linda Argote
| education          = [[Tulane University]]<br>[[University of Michigan]] ([[PhD]])
| workplaces         = [[Carnegie Mellon University]]
| thesis_title       = Input uncertainty and organizational problem solving in hospital emergency service units
| thesis_year        = 1979
| thesis_url         = https://www.proquest.com/docview/302948837
| spouse             = 
}}

'''Linda Marie Argote'''<ref>{{Cite web |last=Argote |first=Linda Marie |title=Input uncertainty and organizational problem solving in hospital emergency service units |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/302948837 |date=1979 |id={{ProQuest|302948837}} }}</ref> is an American academic specializing in [[industrial and organizational psychology]]. She is Thomas Lord Professor of Organizational Behavior and Theory<ref>{{Cite web|last=University|first=Carnegie Mellon|date=|title=Personal Mention - The Piper - Carnegie Mellon University|url=http://www.cmu.edu/piper/news/archives/2021/march/march-25-personals.html|access-date=2021-11-03|website=www.cmu.edu|language=en}}</ref> in the [[Tepper School of Business]] at [[Carnegie Mellon University]], where she directs the Center of Organizational Learning, Innovation and Knowledge.

In 2023, Argote was elected a [[Fellow of the American Association for the Advancement of Science]] (AAAS). Argote served as the Senior Associate Dean for Faculty and Research at the Tepper School from 2020-2022.<ref>{{Cite web|last=University|first=Carnegie Mellon|date=|title=Linda Argote Appointed Senior Associate Dean for Faculty and Research - Tepper School of Business - Carnegie Mellon University|url=https://www.cmu.edu/tepper/news/stories/2020/july/linda-argote-associate-dean.html|access-date=2021-11-03|website=www.cmu.edu|language=en}}</ref>

Argote has courtesy appointments at the [[Heinz College]] and the Department of Social and Decision Sciences at Carnegie Mellon University.

==Education and career==
Argote graduated in 1975 with ''[[magna cum laude]]'' honors from [[Tulane University]], majoring in psychology.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2020-08-05|title=Funding Research|url=https://college.tulane.edu/stories/the-collegian-2020/funding-research|access-date=2021-11-03|website=Newcomb-Tulane College|language=en|archive-date=2021-11-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211103015605/https://college.tulane.edu/stories/the-collegian-2020/funding-research|url-status=dead}}</ref> She completed her Ph.D. in organizational psychology at the [[University of Michigan]] in 1979, and in the same year joined the Carnegie Mellon University faculty. She has also held a faculty appointment at [[Northwestern University]] and was a visiting scholar at [[Stanford University]], the [[University of Pittsburgh]]’s Learning Research and Development Center, and [[Aarhus University]].  She was the Montzemolo Visiting Professor at [[Cambridge Judge Business School|Judge Business School]] of the [[University of Cambridge]], where she was also a Visiting Fellow at [[Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge|Sidney Sussex College]].

Argote published in the field of organizational learning and knowledge transfer.  Her research involves how groups and organizations learn, how they retain the knowledge that they acquire and how they transfer it within and between organizational units.  She and her coauthors conducted a series of studies aimed at understanding the variation observed in rates of learning in firms.  A particular focus of this work was knowledge transfer, or the extent to which lessons learned in one organizational unit transferred to other units.<ref name="Argote, Epple & Beckman, 1990">{{cite journal |last1=Argote |first1=Linda |last2=Beckman |first2=S. |last3=Epple |first3=Dennis |title=The Persistence and Transfer of Learning in Industrial Settings |journal=Management Science |date=1990 |volume=36 |issue=2 |pages=140–154|doi=10.1287/mnsc.36.2.140 }}</ref><ref name="Darr, Argote & Epple, 1995">{{cite journal |last1=Darr |first1=E. |last2=Argote |first2=Linda |last3=Epple |first3=Dennis |title=The Acquisition, Transfer, and Depreciation of Knowledge in Service Organizations: Productivity in Franchises |journal=Management Science |date=1995 |volume=41 |issue=11 |pages=1750–1762|doi=10.1287/mnsc.41.11.1750 |s2cid=15798155 }}</ref><ref name="Egelman, Epple, Argote & Fuchs, 2017">{{cite journal |last1=Egelman |first1=C. |last2=Epple |first2=Dennis|last3=Argote |first3=Linda |last4=Fuchs |first4=E. R. H. |title=Learning by Doing in Multi-Product Manufacturing: Variety, Customizations and Overlapping Product Generations |journal=Management Science |date=2017 |volume=63 |issue=2 |pages=405–423|doi=10.1287/mnsc.2015.2352 }}</ref><ref name="Epple, Argote & Devadas, 1991">{{cite journal |last1=Epple |first1=D. |last2=Argote |first2=L. |last3=Devadas |first3=R. |title=Organizational Learning Curves: A Method for Investigating Intra-Plant Transfer of Knowledge Acquired Through Learning by Doing |journal=Organization Science |date=1991 |volume=2 |pages=58–70|doi=10.1287/orsc.2.1.58 }}</ref><ref name="Epple, Argote & Murphy, 1996">{{cite journal |last1=Epple |first1=D. |last2=Argote |first2=L. |last3=Murphy |first3=K. |title=An Empirical Investigation of the Micro Structure of Knowledge Acquisition and Transfer through Learning by Doing.  |journal=Operations Research |date=1996 |volume=44 |pages=77–86|doi=10.1287/opre.44.1.77 }}</ref> Her article with Eric Darr and [[Dennis Epple]], “The Acquisition, Transfer and Depreciation of Knowledge in Service Organizations: Productivity in Franchises,” was recognized as one of the most influential articles published in ''[[Management Science (journal)|Management Science]]'' during its first 50 years.<ref name="Hopp, 2004">{{cite journal |last1=Hopp |first1=W. J. |title=Fifty years of Management Science |journal=Management Science |date=January 2004 |volume=50 |issue=1 |pages=1–7 |doi=10.1287/mnsc.1030.0181 |url=https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/abs/10.1287/mnsc.1030.0181|url-access=subscription }}</ref>  Argote reviewed research on knowledge transfer and provided a framework for predicting when knowledge transfer is likely to be successful in the ''Annual Review of Psychology'' in 2024. With Ray Reagans and Daria Brooks, Argote examined organizational [[Experience curve effects|learning curves]] for hospital surgical procedures and determined the contribution of the experience of individual team members, of the experience team members acquired working together and of the experience of the hospital towards organizational learning.<ref name="Reagans, Argote & Brooks, 2005">{{cite journal |last1=Reagans |first1=Ray |last2=Argote |first2=Linda |last3=Brooks |first3=D. |title=Individual Experience and Experience Working Together: Predicting Learning Rates from Knowing Who Knows What and Knowing How to Work Together |journal=Management Science |date=2005 |volume=51 |issue=6 |pages=869–881|doi=10.1287/mnsc.1050.0366 |s2cid=12366530 }}</ref>

In collaboration with Paul Ingram, Argote developed the implications of knowledge transfer, or learning from the experience of others, for firm competitive advantage. Their article, “Knowledge Transfer in Organizations: A Basis for Competitive Advantage in Firms,” was recognized as one of the most influential articles published in ''[[Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes]]'' during its first 50 years.<ref name="Chen, 2016">{{cite journal |last1=Chen |first1=X. |title=Celebrating Fifty Years of Organizational Behavior and Decision Research (1966-2016) |journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes |date=September 2016 |volume=136 |pages=1–2|doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2016.09.002 }}</ref>

Argote and Richard Moreland conducted a series of experiments on the concept of [[transactive memory]], or knowledge of who knows what. An initial study found that groups with strong transactive memories recalled more and performed better on a subsequent task than groups with weak transactive memories.<ref name="Liang, Moreland & Argote, 1995">{{cite journal |last1=Liang |first1=D. |last2=Moreland |first2=R. |last3=Argote |first3=Linda |title=Group versus Individual Training and Group-Performance: The Mediating Role of Transactive Memory |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |date=1995 |volume=21 |issue=4 |pages=384–393|doi=10.1177/0146167295214009 |s2cid=145473164 }}</ref> Transactive memories have also been found to foster innovation<ref name="Gino, Argote, Miron-Spektor & Todorova, 2010">{{cite journal |last1=Gino |first1=F. |last2=Argote |first2=L. |last3=Miron-Spektor |first3=E. |last4=Todorova |first4=G. |title=First Get Your Feet Wet: When and Why Prior Experience Fosters Team Creativity |journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes |date=2010 |volume=111 |issue=2 |pages=93–101 |doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2009.11.001 |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/organizational-behavior-and-human-decision-processes/vol/111/issue/2|url-access=subscription }}</ref> and be especially valuable on non-routine or uncertain tasks.<ref name="Ren, Carley & Argote, 2006">{{cite journal |last1=Ren |first1=Y. |last2=Carley |first2=K. M. |last3=Argote |first3=L. |title=The Contingent Effects of Transactive Memory: When is it More Beneficial to Know What Others Know? |journal=Management Science |date=2006 |volume=52 |issue=5 |pages=671–682 |doi=10.1287/mnsc.1050.0496 |url=http://mansci.journal.informs.org/content/52/5/671.abstract |url-access=subscription }}{{Dead link|date=December 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }}</ref>  Ren and Argote (2011)<ref name="Ren and Argote, 2011">{{cite journal |last1=Ren |first1=Y |last2=Argote |first2=L. |title=Transactive memory systems 1985-2010: An Integrative Framework of Key Dimensions, Antecedents and Consequences |journal=Academy of Management Annals |date=2011 |volume=5 |pages=189–230 |doi=10.1080/19416520.2011.590300 |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/19416520.2011.590300|url-access=subscription }}</ref> reviewed the literature on transactive memory. Argote and Ren (2012)<ref name="Argote and Ren, 2012">{{cite journal |last1=Argote |first1=L. |last2=Ren |first2=Y. |title=Transactive Memory Systems: A Micro Foundations of Dynamic Capabilities |journal=Journal of Management Studies |date=2012 |volume=49 |issue=8 |pages=1375–1382 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-6486.2012.01077.x |url=https://www.dropbox.com/s/ur0hjmolqbbbimu/Ren%20Journal%20J%20of%20Management%20Studies%202012.pdf?dl=0
}}</ref> developed a framework characterizing how transactive memory provides micro foundations for dynamic capabilities in firms.

Argote and her colleagues have investigated factors in addition to transactive memory that provide micro foundations for [[organizational learning]], memory and knowledge transfer. These factors include communication networks,<ref name="Argote, Aven & Kush, 2018">{{cite journal |last1=Argote |first1=L. |last2=Aven |first2=B. |last3=Kush |first3=J. A. |title=The Effects of Communication Networks and Turnover on Transactive Memory and Group Performance |journal=Organization Science |date=2018 |volume=29 |issue=2 |pages=191–201 |doi=10.1287/orsc.2017.1176 |url=https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/abs/10.1287/orsc.2017.1176|url-access=subscription }}</ref> work group structure,<ref name="Fahrenkopf, Guo and Argote, 2020">{{cite journal |last1=Fahrenkopf |first1=E. |last2=Guo |first2=J. M. |last3=Argote |first3=L. |title=Personnel Mobility and Organizational Performance: The Effects of Specialist vs. Generalist Experience and Organization Work Structure |journal=Organization Science |date=2020 |volume=31 |issue=6 |pages=1601–1620|doi=10.1287/orsc.2020.1373 |s2cid=225140802 }}</ref> member turnover,<ref name="Rao & Argote, 2006">{{cite journal |last1=Rao |first1=R. |last2=Argote |first2=L. |title=Organizational Learning and Forgetting: The Effects of Turnover and Structure |journal=European Management Review |date=2006 |volume=3 |issue=2 |pages=77–85 |doi=10.1057/palgrave.emr.1500057 |s2cid=144842818 |url=http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1057/palgrave.emr.1500057/abstract|url-access=subscription }}</ref> social identity,<ref name="Kane, Argote & Levine, 2005">{{cite journal |last1=Kane |first1=A. A. |last2=Argote |first2=L. |last3=Levine |first3=J. M. |title=Knowledge Transfer Between Groups via Personnel Rotation: Effects of Social Identity and Knowledge Quality |journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes |date=2005 |volume=96 |issue=1 |pages=56–71 |doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2004.09.002 |url=http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0749597804000779|url-access=subscription }}</ref> and emotion.<ref name="HaKonsson, Eskildsen, Argote, Monster, Burton & Obel, 2016">{{cite journal |last1=Hakonsson |first1=D. D. |last2=Eskildsen |first2=J. K. |last3=Argote |first3=L. |last4=Monster |first4=D. |last5=Burton |first5=R. M. |last6=Obel |first6=B. |title=Exploration versus Exploitation: Emotions and Performance as Antecedents and Consequences of Team Decisions |journal=Strategic Management Journal |date=2016 |volume=37 |issue=6 |pages=985–1001 |doi=10.1002/smj.2380 |s2cid=167475695 |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/smj.2380}}</ref>

Argote and Levine (2020)<ref name="Argote and Levine (2020)">{{cite book |last1=Argote |first1=L. |last2=Levine |first2=J. M. |title=Handbook of Group and Organizational Learning |date=2020 |publisher=Oxford University Press}}</ref>  integrated research at the organizational level with more micro studies at the group level in the ''Oxford Handbook of Group and Organizational Learning''. Argote and Miron-Spektor (2011)<ref name="Argote and Miron-Spektor, 2011">{{cite book |last1=Argote |first1=L. |last2=Miron-Spektor |first2=E. |title=Proceedings of the 2020 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems |chapter=Learning Through Exploration: How Children, Adults, and Older Adults Interact with a New Feature-Rich Application |date=2011 |volume=22 |pages=1123–1137 |doi=10.1145/3313831.3376414 |hdl=2429/72137 |isbn=978-1-4503-6708-0 |s2cid=218482768 |chapter-url=https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1145/3313831.3376414|hdl-access=free }}</ref> developed a theoretical framework for analyzing organizational learning. Argote, Lee and Park (2021)<ref name="Argote, Lee and Park, 2021">{{cite journal |last1=Argote |first1=L. |last2=Lee |first2=S. |last3=Park |first3=J. |title=Organizational Learning Processes and Outcomes: Major Findings and Future Research Directions |journal=Management Science |date=2021 |volume=67 |issue=9 |pages=5399–5429|doi=10.1287/mnsc.2020.3693 |s2cid=228979872 }}</ref> reviewed the literature on organizational learning and its subprocesses of search and knowledge creation, retention and transfer.

Argote synthesized her work and related research in ''Organizational Learning:  Creating, Retaining and Transferring Knowledge'' (Kluwer, 1999; 2nd ed, Springer, 2013).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lant |first1=Theresa K. |title=Review of ''Organizational Learning'' |journal=Administrative Science Quarterly |date=September 2000 |volume=45 |issue=3 |doi=10.2307/2667112 | jstor = 2667112|s2cid=220642541 }}</ref> Her book was a finalist for the [http://aom.org/membership/awards-and-recognition/george-r.-terry-book-award George R. Terry Book Award] of the [[Academy of Management]].

She was the editor-in-chief of the journal ''[[Organization Science (journal)|Organization Science]]'' from 2004 to 2010.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Editorial Board {{!}} Organization Science|url=https://pubsonline.informs.org/page/orsc/editorial-board|access-date=2021-11-03|website=pubsonline.informs.org|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Gale - Institution Finder|url=https://galeapps.gale.com/apps/auth?userGroupName=&origURL=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.gale.com%2Fps%2Fi.do%3Fid%3DGALE%257CA114362786&prodId=GVRL|access-date=2021-11-03|website=galeapps.gale.com}}</ref> She also served as Departmental Editor at ''Management Science'' (1995-2002).

==Recognition==
Argote became David M. Kirr and Barbara A. Kirr Professor in 1997.

She is a [[Fellow]] of the following academic associations:

* [https://www.aaas.org/page/2023-fellows American Association for the Advancement of Science] (AAAS) (elected 2023)
* [[Association for Psychological Science]] (elected 2006). [[:Category:Fellows of the Association for Psychological Science|Full list of APS Fellows]].
* [[Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences]] (2008 class)<ref name="Informs">{{cite web |title=Fellows: Alphabetical List |url=https://www.informs.org/Recognizing-Excellence/Fellows/Fellows-Alphabetical-List |publisher=[[Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences]] |access-date=2021-08-03}}</ref>
* [[Academy of Management]] (elected 2013)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Leadership Directory|url=https://my.aom.org/leadership/toc.aspx?action=AFFIL&code=FELLOWS|access-date=2021-11-03|website=my.aom.org}}</ref>

In 2018 the Interdisciplinary Network for Group Research gave her their Joseph E. McGrath Award for Lifetime Achievement in the Study of Groups.<ref>{{Cite web|title=INGRoup {{!}} Awards|url=http://www.ingroup.net/awards.html#past|access-date=2021-11-03|website=www.ingroup.net}}</ref>

Distinguished Scholar, Technology Management Section (TMS) Division of the Academy of Management<ref>{{Cite web|title=Distinguished Scholar Award - Organization and Management Theory|url=https://omt.aom.org/awards/distinguished-scholar-award|access-date=2021-11-03|website=omt.aom.org|language=en}}</ref>                                                   

Distinguished Speaker Award, Technology Management, Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences ([[Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences|INFORMS]]), 2011<ref>{{Cite web|last=INFORMS|title=INFORMS News: Subdivision Awards|url=https://www.informs.org/ORMS-Today/Public-Articles/February-Volume-39-Number-1/INFORMS-News-Subdivision-Awards|access-date=2021-11-03|website=INFORMS|language=en-US}}</ref>

Distinguished Scholar, Organization and Management Theory Division of the Academy of Management (OMT) 2012<ref>{{Cite web|last=University|first=Carnegie Mellon|date=|title=Personal Mention - The Piper - Carnegie Mellon University|url=http://www.cmu.edu/piper/news/archives/2012/july/july-12/personal-mention.html|access-date=2021-11-03|website=www.cmu.edu|language=en}}</ref>                                        

Distinguished Scholar, Knowledge and Innovation Interest Group, [[Strategic Management Society]], 2013

Honorary Doctorate, Aarhus University, 2019<ref>{{Cite web|last=79697@au.dk|title=Honorary Doctorate for Linda Argote|url=https://mgmt.au.dk/nyheder/nyheder/news-item/artikel/honorary-doctorate-for-linda-argote/|access-date=2021-11-03|website=mgmt.au.dk|date=29 October 2021|language=en|archive-date=2021-11-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211103015555/https://mgmt.au.dk/nyheder/nyheder/news-item/artikel/honorary-doctorate-for-linda-argote/|url-status=dead}}</ref>

Honorary Doctorate, Universita della Svizzera Italiana, 2020<ref>{{Cite web|title=L'USI conferisce il dottorato honoris causa a Linda Argote|url=https://www.usi.ch/it/feeds/13541|access-date=2021-11-03|website=www.usi.ch|language=en}}</ref>

==Selected publications==

* Argote, L.  (2024).  Knowledge transfer within organizations:  Mechanisms, motivation and consideration. ''Annual Review of Psychology'', ''75''(1), 405-431.
* Argote, L., & Ingram, P. (2000). Knowledge transfer: A basis for competitive advantage in firms. ''Organizational behavior and human decision processes'', ''82''(1), 150-169. https://doi.org/10.1006/obhd.2000.2893
* Argote, L. (2012). ''Organizational learning: Creating, retaining and transferring knowledge''. Springer Science & Business Media. 
* Argote, L., McEvily, B., & Reagans, R. (2003). Managing knowledge in organizations: An integrative framework and review of emerging themes. ''Management science'', ''49''(4), 571-582. https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.49.4.571.14424
* Argote, L., & Miron-Spektor, E. (2011). Organizational learning: From experience to knowledge. ''Organization science'', ''22''(5), 1123-1137. https://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.1100.0621
